concept: Criterion / Standard for a Judgment or Classification
description: A stated feature or test used to decide whether something belongs to a category or meets a requirement. A criterion can be necessary, sufficient, both, or neither, and its authority has to be stated rather than assumed.
sound_symbolism: Crisp 'ki' begins with a clean closure and a narrow bright vowel. In stressed 're', the tongue tests a small repeated contact instead of holding still. The lips meet for 'ma' and open onto the broadest vowel. A fine edge passes through two tongue contacts into the broad finish.
grammatical_notes: A countable noun: 'ha kirema noetha nai.' (This criterion is essential.) Use 'natheri' in the appropriate direction to distinguish necessary from sufficient relations rather than treating those labels as interchangeable. Distinct from 'masue' (measure), which compares extent, and 'keiro' (way), which names a method rather than a standard of application.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Criteria make category boundaries visible while allowing speakers to examine how those boundaries depend on purposes and changing conditions.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Stating a criterion prevents hidden standards from being imposed as though every participant had already accepted them.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: A criterion may be embodied in skilled practice, seasonal knowledge, or observed fit as well as in numerical measurement.

tags.cognition: a standard used in classification or judgment
tags.wisdom: an explicit and revisable basis for deciding fit
